source: Packet KEY Motion and Forces CP.pdf

concepts.learning_target: Describe and measure motion using distance, displacement, speed, velocity, and reference points.
concepts.student_friendly_rule: Motion means position changes compared with a reference point.
concepts.needs_router_support: Classify distance vs displacement; speed/velocity math; graph interpretation; direction words.

concepts.learning_target: Find total path distance and straight-line displacement.
concepts.student_friendly_rule: Distance adds the path; displacement compares start to finish and needs direction.
concepts.needs_router_support: Catch round-trip problems as displacement 0; use Pythagorean theorem for perpendicular paths; return compass direction when possible.

concepts.student_friendly_rule: On a distance-time graph, slope means speed.
concepts.needs_router_support: Horizontal line = stopped; straight upward line = constant speed; curve getting steeper = speeding up; curve flattening = slowing down.

concepts.learning_target: Calculate and explain acceleration as change in velocity over time.
concepts.student_friendly_rule: Acceleration happens when speed or direction changes.
concepts.needs_router_support: Handle 'comes to a stop', 'from rest', 'final speed', negative acceleration, and unit consistency.

concepts.student_friendly_rule: On a velocity-time graph, slope means acceleration.
concepts.needs_router_support: Horizontal nonzero line = constant velocity/zero acceleration; upward line = positive acceleration; downward line = negative acceleration; line at zero = not moving.

concepts.learning_target: Determine net force and explain how balanced/unbalanced forces affect motion.
concepts.student_friendly_rule: Balanced forces make 0 N net force; unbalanced forces change motion.
concepts.needs_router_support: Handle force diagrams and words like right/left/up/down; add same-direction forces; subtract opposite-direction forces.

concepts.learning_target: Identify inertia examples and explain objects resisting changes in motion.
concepts.student_friendly_rule: Objects keep doing what they are doing unless an unbalanced force acts.
concepts.needs_router_support: Scenario classification for seatbelts, objects at rest, objects continuing forward, friction stopping motion.

concepts.learning_target: Explain friction types and air resistance effects.
concepts.student_friendly_rule: Friction opposes motion; air resistance depends a lot on shape and surface area.
concepts.needs_router_support: Classify static/sliding/rolling friction; compare falling flat vs crumpled objects; explain terminal velocity.

concepts.learning_target: Use gravity to explain falling objects and calculate weight.
concepts.student_friendly_rule: Weight is the force of gravity on mass; near Earth use 9.8 m/s².
concepts.needs_router_support: Do not overexplain mass vs weight unless asked; calculate W=mg and m=W/g; answer gravity-near-Earth as 9.8 m/s² when asked.

concepts.learning_target: Use F=ma to solve for force, mass, or acceleration.
concepts.student_friendly_rule: More force gives more acceleration; more mass gives less acceleration if force stays the same.
concepts.needs_router_support: Formula router needs forward and rearranged versions of F=ma; net force can come from diagram first, then acceleration = Fnet/m.

concepts.learning_target: Identify equal and opposite action-reaction force pairs.
concepts.student_friendly_rule: Forces come in pairs on different objects.
concepts.needs_router_support: Scenario classification for trampoline, swimming, balloon rockets, hoses, collisions; warn that paired forces act on different objects.

concepts.learning_target: Calculate momentum and apply simple conservation of momentum.
concepts.student_friendly_rule: Momentum equals mass times velocity, and it can transfer in collisions.
concepts.needs_router_support: Formula router for p=mv; scenario classification for collisions and pool balls; simple transfer all momentum problems.

vocabulary.student_definition: A change in an object's position compared with a reference point.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Thinking an object is moving without saying what it is moving compared to.
vocabulary.example_cue: The car changed position compared with the school.

vocabulary.student_definition: The place or object used to tell whether something has changed position.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Forgetting that motion depends on what you compare it to.
vocabulary.example_cue: The locker room can be the reference point for a runner.

vocabulary.student_definition: The total length of the path traveled, no matter what direction the object moved.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Using the straight-line change instead of adding the whole path.
vocabulary.example_cue: Walk 20 m north and 20 m south: distance is 40 m.

vocabulary.student_definition: How far and in what direction an object ends from where it started.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Treating displacement like total distance; leaving off direction.
vocabulary.example_cue: Walk 20 m north and 20 m south: displacement is 0 m.

vocabulary.student_definition: How fast something moves; distance divided by time.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Adding direction to speed; confusing speed with velocity.
vocabulary.example_cue: A runner travels 100 m in 20 s, so speed is 5 m/s.

vocabulary.student_definition: The total distance traveled divided by the total time for the whole trip.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Using only one short part of a trip instead of the whole trip.
vocabulary.example_cue: Average speed over a trip = total distance ÷ total time.

vocabulary.student_definition: The speed of an object at one specific moment.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Thinking it must match the average speed for the whole trip.
vocabulary.example_cue: A speedometer shows instantaneous speed.

vocabulary.student_definition: Speed in a specific direction.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Leaving off direction; treating velocity as only speed.
vocabulary.example_cue: 20 m/s north is a velocity.

vocabulary.student_definition: The rate that velocity changes over time. It can happen by speeding up, slowing down, or changing direction.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Thinking acceleration only means speeding up.
vocabulary.example_cue: A car going from 10 m/s to 30 m/s in 5 s accelerates.

vocabulary.student_definition: Acceleration in the positive direction, often shown as speeding up in classroom examples.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Thinking positive always means good or upward instead of direction/sign.
vocabulary.example_cue: Velocity increases from 2 m/s to 8 m/s.

vocabulary.student_definition: Acceleration in the negative direction; in basic examples this often means slowing down.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Forgetting negative values are expected when an object slows down.
vocabulary.example_cue: Velocity decreases from 20 m/s to 0 m/s.

vocabulary.student_definition: A push or pull that can change an object's motion.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Thinking a force is needed to keep motion going when no friction exists.
vocabulary.example_cue: Pushing a box applies a force.

vocabulary.student_definition: The overall force after all forces on an object are combined.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Adding opposite forces instead of subtracting them; ignoring direction.
vocabulary.example_cue: 7 N right and 3 N left gives 4 N right.

vocabulary.student_definition: Forces that cancel out so the net force is 0 N and motion does not change.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Thinking no forces are acting just because the object is not accelerating.
vocabulary.example_cue: A book resting on a table has balanced forces.

vocabulary.student_definition: Forces that do not cancel out, causing a change in motion.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Forgetting that direction matters when finding net force.
vocabulary.example_cue: A stronger push to the right than left makes the object accelerate right.

vocabulary.student_definition: An object's tendency to resist a change in motion.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Thinking faster objects always have more inertia; inertia mainly depends on mass.
vocabulary.example_cue: A heavy truck is harder to start or stop than a bicycle.

vocabulary.student_definition: A force that opposes motion between surfaces that touch.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Thinking friction always stops motion instantly or only happens when an object is moving.
vocabulary.example_cue: Grass slows down a kicked soccer ball.

vocabulary.student_definition: Friction that keeps an object from starting to move.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Forgetting that friction can act before an object slides.
vocabulary.example_cue: A box does not move when a small push is applied.

vocabulary.student_definition: Friction between surfaces that are sliding past each other.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Confusing sliding friction with rolling friction.
vocabulary.example_cue: A book sliding across a desk slows down.

vocabulary.student_definition: Friction on an object that rolls over a surface.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Thinking rolling friction is usually stronger than sliding friction in basic examples.
vocabulary.example_cue: A ball rolling across the floor slows down.

vocabulary.student_definition: A friction-like force from air that opposes an object's motion through the air.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Ignoring shape and surface area when comparing falling objects.
vocabulary.example_cue: A flat sheet of paper falls slower than a crumpled paper ball.

vocabulary.student_definition: An attractive force between objects with mass.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Thinking heavier objects fall faster when air resistance is ignored.
vocabulary.example_cue: Earth pulls objects downward.

vocabulary.student_definition: All objects with mass attract each other; the strength depends on mass and distance.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Thinking gravity only exists on Earth.
vocabulary.example_cue: A satellite stays in orbit because of Earth's gravity.

vocabulary.student_definition: The constant speed reached when air resistance balances the force of gravity on a falling object.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Thinking the object stops falling when forces balance; it keeps moving at constant speed.
vocabulary.example_cue: A skydiver eventually falls at constant speed before opening the parachute.

vocabulary.student_definition: The force of gravity on an object's mass.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Confusing weight with mass; weight is measured in newtons.
vocabulary.example_cue: A 10 kg object weighs about 98 N on Earth.

vocabulary.student_definition: The amount of matter in an object; in this unit it affects inertia, acceleration, weight, and momentum.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Treating mass and weight as the same measurement.
vocabulary.example_cue: Mass is measured in kilograms.

vocabulary.student_definition: A measure of how hard it is to stop a moving object, found by multiplying mass by velocity.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Thinking only speed matters; mass matters too.
vocabulary.example_cue: A heavy truck moving fast has a lot of momentum.

vocabulary.student_definition: In a collision, momentum can transfer between objects, but total momentum stays the same if no outside force acts.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Thinking momentum disappears in a collision instead of transferring or changing forms with outside effects.
vocabulary.example_cue: A cue ball transfers momentum to an 8 ball.

vocabulary.student_definition: An object at rest stays at rest and an object in motion stays in motion unless acted on by an unbalanced force.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Thinking moving objects need a continued force even without friction.
vocabulary.example_cue: A passenger keeps moving forward when a bike stops suddenly.

vocabulary.student_definition: Acceleration depends on net force and mass; force equals mass times acceleration.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Forgetting that more mass means less acceleration if force stays the same.
vocabulary.example_cue: A lighter object accelerates more than a heavier object with the same force.

vocabulary.student_definition: For every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force.
vocabulary.common_misconceptions: Thinking action and reaction forces act on the same object; they act on different objects.
vocabulary.example_cue: Air moves backward from a balloon, and the balloon moves forward.

formulas.variables: s speed, d distance, t time
formulas.trigger_phrases: speed; how fast; distance in time; how long; how far
formulas.student_steps: Identify distance and time; divide distance by time; include units.

formulas.variables: d distance, s speed, t time
formulas.trigger_phrases: how far; travels at speed for time; cover ground
formulas.student_steps: Multiply speed by time; keep time units consistent with speed units.

formulas.variables: t time, d distance, s speed
formulas.trigger_phrases: how long; time it takes; travel distance at speed
formulas.student_steps: Divide distance by speed; include time units.

formulas.variables: v velocity, d displacement/distance in a direction, t time
formulas.student_steps: Find speed, then attach the correct direction.

formulas.variables: a one direction leg, b perpendicular leg, c straight-line displacement
formulas.trigger_phrases: east then north; west then south; straight-line displacement; resultant
formulas.student_steps: Add path for distance; use Pythagorean theorem for displacement; include compass direction.

formulas.variables: a acceleration, vf final velocity, vi initial velocity, t time
formulas.rearrangements: vf = vi + a*t; vi = vf - a*t; t = (vf-vi)/a
formulas.trigger_phrases: accelerates from; speeds up from; slows down from; comes to a stop; acceleration
formulas.student_steps: Subtract initial velocity from final velocity; divide by time; negative means slowing or opposite direction.

formulas.variables: vf final velocity, vi initial velocity, a acceleration, t time
formulas.trigger_phrases: final speed; reaches what speed; starting from rest; accelerates for
formulas.student_steps: Multiply acceleration by time; add initial velocity.

formulas.variables: F net force, m mass, a acceleration
formulas.trigger_phrases: force; net force; mass and acceleration; Newton's second law
formulas.student_steps: Multiply mass by acceleration; answer in newtons.

formulas.variables: m mass, F force, a acceleration
formulas.trigger_phrases: what mass; force and acceleration; object experiencing net force
formulas.student_steps: Divide force by acceleration; answer in kilograms.

formulas.variables: a acceleration, F net force, m mass
formulas.trigger_phrases: what acceleration; horizontal force applied; mass and force
formulas.student_steps: Divide net force by mass; answer in m/s^2.

formulas.variables: W weight, m mass, g gravitational field strength near Earth
formulas.trigger_phrases: weigh; weight; falling object; force of gravity; mass from weight
formulas.student_steps: Use g = 9.8 m/s^2 near Earth unless the problem gives another value; multiply mass by 9.8.

formulas.variables: p momentum, m mass, v velocity
formulas.trigger_phrases: momentum; mass and velocity; moving object; collision
formulas.student_steps: Multiply mass by velocity; include kg*m/s.

formulas.variables: m mass, p momentum, v velocity
formulas.trigger_phrases: mass from momentum; has momentum when thrown with velocity

formulas.variables: v velocity, p momentum, m mass
formulas.trigger_phrases: speed from momentum; how fast; momentum and mass

formulas.solve_for: post-collision velocity in simple transfer problems
formulas.variables: m1 first mass, v1 first velocity, m2 second mass, v2 second velocity
formulas.trigger_phrases: transfers all momentum; collision; stationary object; conservation of momentum
formulas.student_steps: Find initial momentum; set it equal to final momentum of second object; solve for final velocity.

formulas.rearrangements: Use signed values or subtract smaller from larger and keep direction of larger force.
formulas.trigger_phrases: net force; forces acting left and right; balanced; unbalanced; diagram
formulas.student_steps: Add forces in the same direction; subtract opposite directions; direction points toward the greater force.

formulas.variables: distance marker, average time from trials
formulas.trigger_phrases: runner lab; average times; distance marker; velocity calculation
formulas.student_steps: Average trial times first; divide distance by average time for each marker.

formulas.variables: F force, m rocket mass, a acceleration
formulas.trigger_phrases: balloon rocket; mass affects acceleration and force; lab calculations
formulas.student_steps: Calculate speed and acceleration from data, then multiply mass by acceleration.

graphPatterns.ai_response_rule: Say speed is 0 because distance does not change as time passes.

graphPatterns.meaning: object moves at constant speed away from the reference point

graphPatterns.ai_response_rule: The slope increases, so the speed increases.
The slope decreases, so the speed decreases.

graphPatterns.visual_pattern: line slopes downward toward zero distance
graphPatterns.meaning: object returns toward the reference point
graphPatterns.ai_response_rule: Distance from the start is decreasing, so the object is heading back.
Velocity is 0 the whole time, so acceleration is also 0.
Velocity stays the same, so acceleration is 0.

graphPatterns.meaning: positive acceleration / speeding up in basic examples
graphPatterns.ai_response_rule: Velocity increases over time, so acceleration is positive.

graphPatterns.meaning: negative acceleration / slowing down in basic examples
graphPatterns.ai_response_rule: Velocity decreases over time, so acceleration is negative.

problemBank.question: A student walks 40 m east, then 15 m west. Find the distance and displacement.
problemBank.worked_steps: Distance adds the path: 40 + 15 = 55 m. Displacement compares start to finish: 40 east - 15 west = 25 m east.

problemBank.question: A runner completes one full lap around a 400 m track and ends where she started. Find distance and displacement.
problemBank.worked_steps: Distance is the full path traveled. Since start and finish are the same point, displacement is 0 m.

problemBank.question: A robot drives 6 m north and then 8 m east. Find distance and straight-line displacement.
problemBank.worked_steps: Distance = 6 + 8 = 14 m. Displacement = sqrt(6^2 + 8^2) = 10 m toward northeast.

problemBank.question: A cart travels 36 m in 6 s. What is its speed?
A bike travels 18 km north in 0.5 hr. What is its velocity?
problemBank.worked_steps: v = d/t = 18 km / 0.5 hr = 36 km/hr, then include north.

problemBank.question: A car moves at 55 mi/hr for 2 hours. How far does it travel?
problemBank.worked_steps: d = s*t = 55 mi/hr * 2 hr = 110 mi.

problemBank.question: Sound travels about 343 m/s. About how long does it take sound to travel 686 m?
On a distance-time graph, an object moves from 0 m at 0 s to 20 m at 5 s in a straight line. What is its speed?
problemBank.worked_steps: Speed is slope on a distance-time graph: rise/run = 20 m / 5 s = 4 m/s.

problemBank.question: A distance-time graph has a horizontal line. What does that show?
problemBank.expected_answer: The object is stopped/not moving; speed is 0.
problemBank.worked_steps: Distance does not change while time passes, so the object is not moving.

problemBank.question: A distance-time graph curves upward and gets steeper over time. What does that show?
problemBank.worked_steps: On a distance-time graph, slope means speed. If the slope gets steeper, speed increases.

problemBank.question: A scooter speeds up from 4 m/s to 16 m/s in 3 s. What is its acceleration?
A skateboarder slows from 12 m/s to 0 m/s in 4 s. What is the acceleration?
problemBank.worked_steps: a = (0 - 12)/4 = -3 m/s^2. Negative shows the velocity decreased.

problemBank.question: A cart starts at 3 m/s and accelerates at 2 m/s^2 for 5 s. What final velocity does it reach?
A toy car starts from rest and reaches 9 m/s in 3 s. What is its acceleration?
problemBank.worked_steps: From rest means vi = 0. a = (9 - 0)/3 = 3 m/s^2.

problemBank.question: On a velocity-time graph, velocity increases from 0 m/s to 30 m/s in 10 s. What is the acceleration?
problemBank.worked_steps: Acceleration is slope on a velocity-time graph: (30 - 0)/10 = 3 m/s^2.

problemBank.question: A velocity-time graph is a flat horizontal line at 6 m/s. What is the acceleration?
problemBank.worked_steps: Velocity is constant, so the slope is 0. Acceleration is 0 m/s^2.

problemBank.question: List three ways an object can accelerate.
problemBank.expected_answer: It can speed up, slow down, or change direction.
problemBank.worked_steps: Acceleration is any change in velocity. Velocity includes speed and direction.

problemBank.question: Two forces act on a box: 12 N right and 5 N left. What is the net force?
problemBank.worked_steps: Opposite directions subtract: 12 - 5 = 7 N. Direction is right because 12 N is larger.

problemBank.question: Two students push a cart to the right with 20 N each. Another student pushes left with 15 N. What is the net force?
problemBank.worked_steps: Add same direction first: 20 + 20 = 40 N right. Subtract the opposite force: 40 - 15 = 25 N right.

problemBank.question: A box has 10 N pushing right and 10 N pushing left. Are the forces balanced or unbalanced? What is the net force?
problemBank.worked_steps: Equal opposite forces cancel out, so the net force is 0 N and motion does not change.

problemBank.question: A backpack left on the floor stays there until someone picks it up. Which law does this show?
problemBank.worked_steps: An object at rest stays at rest unless an unbalanced force acts on it.

problemBank.question: Rank these from least inertia to most inertia: feather, baseball, bicycle, car.
problemBank.worked_steps: Inertia depends mainly on mass. Rank from smallest mass to largest mass.

problemBank.question: A book is sliding across a table and slows down. What type of friction is acting?
problemBank.worked_steps: The surfaces are moving/sliding past each other, so it is sliding friction.

problemBank.question: A flat paper and a crumpled paper with the same mass are dropped. Which usually lands first and why?
problemBank.expected_answer: The crumpled paper usually lands first because it has less air resistance.
problemBank.worked_steps: Shape changes how much air pushes against the paper. Less air resistance means it falls more like other objects near Earth.

problemBank.question: A 12 kg object accelerates at 4 m/s^2. What net force acts on it?
A 40 N net force acts on a 10 kg cart. What is the acceleration?
An object experiences a 90 N net force and accelerates at 3 m/s^2. What is its mass?
A 5 kg drone speeds up from 2 m/s to 14 m/s in 4 s. What net force caused the change?
problemBank.worked_steps: First find acceleration: a = (14 - 2)/4 = 3 m/s^2. Then F = m*a = 5*3 = 15 N.

problemBank.question: What is the weight of a 25 kg object on Earth?
A falling object has a weight of 196 N near Earth. What is its mass?
A swimmer pushes water backward and moves forward. Which law does this show?
problemBank.worked_steps: The swimmer pushes water backward; the water pushes the swimmer forward with an equal and opposite force.

problemBank.question: In a balloon rocket, air rushes backward out of the balloon and the balloon moves forward. Which force is the reaction force?
problemBank.expected_answer: The balloon moving forward is the reaction to the air being pushed backward.
problemBank.worked_steps: Action: balloon pushes air backward. Reaction: air pushes balloon forward.

problemBank.question: Find the momentum of a 60 kg skateboarder moving at 5 m/s.
problemBank.worked_steps: p = m*v = 60 kg * 5 m/s = 300 kg*m/s.

problemBank.question: An 8 kg object has 40 kg*m/s of momentum. How fast is it moving?
A ball has 18 kg*m/s of momentum and moves at 6 m/s. What is its mass?
A 10 kg cart moving at 2 m/s transfers all its momentum to a 5 kg cart. What is the 5 kg cart's speed after?
problemBank.worked_steps: Initial momentum = 10*2 = 20 kg*m/s. Set 20 = 5*v, so v = 4 m/s.

problemBank.question: A motorcycle accelerates faster than a loaded truck when the same size force is applied. Which law best explains this?
problemBank.worked_steps: With the same force, the lower-mass object has greater acceleration.

problemBank.question: A soccer ball rolls across grass and eventually stops. Which law and force are involved?
problemBank.expected_answer: Newton's 1st Law with friction acting as the unbalanced force.
problemBank.worked_steps: The ball would keep moving, but friction from the grass changes its motion and slows it down.

problemBank.question: Ignoring air resistance, what acceleration should a dropped apple and tennis ball have near Earth?
problemBank.expected_answer: Both accelerate at about 9.8 m/s^2 downward.
problemBank.worked_steps: Near Earth, falling objects have the same acceleration due to gravity if air resistance is ignored.

smokeTests.test_query: what is speed if I go 36 meters in 6 seconds
find volocity 18 km north in .5 hours
smokeTests.notes: Misspelled velocity; direction should be preserved.

smokeTests.test_query: distance vs displacement if I walk 40m east then 15m west
a car goes from 12 m/s to 0 in 4 seconds what is acceleration
smokeTests.notes: Comes to a stop -> final velocity 0.

smokeTests.test_query: from rest to 9 m/s in 3 seconds acceleration
what does a flat line on a distance time graph mean
what does slope mean on a velocity vs time graph
12 N right and 5 N left net force
force for 12 kg accelerating at 4 m/s2
40 N force on 10 kg cart acceleration
how much does 25 kg weigh on earth
momentum of 60 kg skateboarder going 5 m/s
swimmer pushes water back and moves forward what law
why does a flat paper fall slower than crumpled paper
smokeTests.expected_core_answer: flat paper has more air resistance

smokeTests.test_query: rank feather baseball bicycle car by inertia
